name: Platform-neutral governance and procurement framework
consequences: Pulling this lever establishes formal platform-neutral requirements in public procurement and creates governance oversight to enforce them. Direct effect: procurement criteria begin to favor vendor-neutral standards and fallback capabilities, reducing lock-in risk. Downstream: pushes suppliers to align with resilience expectations and increases regulatory scrutiny; potential trade-offs include longer procurement cycles and higher upfront governance overhead, with possible stakeholder pushback from legacy platforms.
options: Establish a formal platform-neutral governance council that mandates fallback authentication references in public procurement, conducts impact assessments, and publishes compliance mappings to eIDAS and NSIS standards.

Embed platform-neutral requirements into all major Digitaliseringsstyrelsen procurement frameworks, require supplier diversification clauses, and link funding releases to evidence of platform independence and continuity planning.

Commission a legally binding procurement pilot with AltID integration language that prioritizes vendor-neutral identity providers, mandates cross-platform interoperability testing, and enforces service-level agreements tied to resilience metrics.
review: Core tension: governance objectives may slow procurement and conflict with incumbent commitments. The options miss a clear escalation path if suppliers resist platform-neutral terms, and they do not specify measurable success criteria or fallback-budget triggers.

name: Fallback-path demonstrators and evidence credibility
consequences: Non-production demonstrators create tangible demonstrations of interoperable, standards-based access without relying on MitID live environments. Direct effect: builds credible evidence for policy arguments and procurement language; downstream: may reveal gaps requiring further standards work or phased deployment, with potential cost and timeline implications.
options: Launch two non-production demonstrators—mobile-based and browser-based/token-based—to collect rigorous interoperability data and produce policy-ready evidence for fallback authentication without integrating live MitID.

Develop a standards-based fallback prototype using FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect, tested on GrapheneOS, to quantify practical interoperability gaps and highlight how a platform-neutral path could operate in parallel.
Organize a neutral verification exercise with third-party auditors to validate security properties of the fallback path and publish a transparent risk-benefit memo for Danish authorities.
review: Core tension: demonstrations may overstate readiness or fail to translate into procurement language. The options lack a plan for integrating findings into binding regulatory or procurement milestones if gaps persist.

name: EU and Danish standards alignment pressure
consequences: Engaging standards bodies aligns AltID with European wallet guidelines and reduces bespoke Danish lock-in. Direct effect: increases interoperability expectations and procurement language coherence; downstream: may slow consensus-building and require compromises with stakeholders who prefer existing platforms.
options: Lead a joint Danish-EU standards task force to harmonize AltID requirements with European Digital Identity Wallet guidelines, producing concrete negotiation points and procurement language for platform-neutrality.
Submit formal comments to EU working groups advocating interoperable fallback mechanisms, ensuring Danish procurement language mirrors European resilience criteria and minimizes lock-in.
Sponsor a public standardization workshop series to map NSIS, eIDAS, and wallet interoperability gaps, generating a shared Danish position paper for later EU consultations.
review: Core tension: EU alignment can create competing priorities with national procurement timelines. The options do not specify how to secure timely political buy-in or how to sequence engagement with industry partners.

name: Independent risk intelligence and supplier concentration mapping
consequences: Mapping supplier concentration exposes resilience gaps and creates a basis for targeted mitigations; downstream: could trigger shifts in budgeting, governance, and procurement with political scrutiny over incumbents who benefit from current arrangements.
options: Commission an independent risk map of supplier concentration in public digital identity, quantify exposure across services, and publish a publicly auditable risk register with mitigation timelines.
Develop a dynamic resilience dashboard that tracks key indicators (availability, policy changes, incidents) for MitID-dependency and potential fallback pathways, updating stakeholders monthly.
Institute a cross-ministerial risk committee that reviews platform dependencies quarterly and mandates contingency planning updates tied to procurement cycles.
review: Core tension: risk mapping may provoke political pushback from incumbents and could be deprioritized if short-term pressures dominate. The options lack a concrete funding mechanism or mandate to act on identified risks.

name: Public communication framing and coalition-building
consequences: Effective framing and coalition-building create legitimacy for platform-neutral paths and reduce resistance from skeptics; downstream: messages must be accurate, and overpromising can lead to credibility gaps if demonstrations lag procurement outcomes.
options: Launch a resilience-focused public communication campaign with civil-society, academia, and media partners to explain concrete continuity benefits of platform-neutral access.
Organize stakeholder roundtables across Folketinget committees, privacy advocates, and operators to co-create policy proposals and preempt resistance from incumbents by demonstrating shared benefits.
Publish a risk-led policy note series detailing continuity-of-service scenarios and how platform-neutral paths guard essential public access during geopolitical disruptions.
review: Core tension: communication can be perceived as political signaling rather than evidence-based. The options should include metrics or milestones to anchor messaging to verifiable progress.

name: Regulatory alignment and privacy-by-design
consequences: Ensuring regulatory coherence and privacy-by-design reduces legal risk and protects public trust; downstream: may require adjustments to data governance and oversight, potentially slowing implementation but improving legitimacy.
options: Draft a formal regulatory impact assessment linking eIDAS/NSIS compliance to procurement neutrality, and present it to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen with timelines and resource needs.
Coordinate with the Danish Data Protection Agency to ensure privacy-by-design for fallback authentication, establishing clear data-use boundaries within platform-neutral solutions.
Negotiate a temporary regulatory sandbox to test fallback authentication provisions without obligating immediate full deployment, enabling iterative learning while managing political risk.
review: Core tension: regulatory processes can become bottlenecks; options miss a plan to parallelize with procurement milestones or specify concrete sandbox criteria and exit conditions.

name: Platform-neutral interoperability charter for identity provisioning
consequences: Adopting a formal platform-neutral interoperability charter standardizes how identity providers exchange data across systems, enabling non-platform-specific authentication paths and reducing vendor lock-in. Direct effect is a shared baseline for protocols, test suites, and procurement criteria that encourage multi-vendor support. Downstream, this creates cross-government coordination burdens and potential slowdowns as stakeholders align on standards and certification requirements.
options: Codify mandatory support for FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect across new identity services, with certified fallback paths tested against open standards.
Launch an independent pilot testing platform-neutral authentication across two public portals, publishing performance and risk findings.
Embed fallback-path criteria in procurement requirements, ensuring suppliers demonstrate resilience to platform outages and policy shifts.
review: Core tension is balancing rapid adoption with comprehensive interoperability certification; a weakness is that pilots and charters may not translate into binding procurement language unless budget and governance are synchronized, risking a gap between policy intent and contract clauses.

name: Resilience framing for continuity-of-service procurement
consequences: Shifting the policy narrative toward continuity and resilience anchors procurement decisions in risk reduction and ongoing citizen access, broadening cross-ministry support. Downstream, incumbents who benefit from current platform dependencies may push back, raising political and budgetary frictions. It also incentivizes the inclusion of diverse trust anchors rather than single-vendor dependencies.
options: Frame procurement discussions around service continuity and user access resilience, securing cross-ministry commitments to diversify trust anchors.
Build a public-private coalition to demand transparent platform-neutral fallback requirements and publish quarterly resilience dashboards.
Design a phased procurement plan that rewards early pilots with scale-up funding contingent on measurable resilience improvements.
review: Core tension is aligning resilience rhetoric with feasible procurement levers; weakness is the risk that coalition rhetoric outpaces budgetary authority, leading to aspirational targets without binding commitments.

name: Independent assurance mechanisms with third-party attestations
consequences: Establish a standing third-party attestation framework for identity provider interoperability and fallback readiness, with published results. This reduces vendor influence by adding external credibility, but increases administrative overhead, cost, and potential delays in procurement cycles as attestations are produced.
options: Establish a standing third-party attestation framework for identity provider interoperability and fallback readiness, with published results.
Require continuous security review by neutral labs during procurement cycles to validate platform-neutral compliance.
Fund open-source reference implementations to accelerate interoperability and lower entry barriers for new entrants.
review: Core tension is balancing external assurance with streamlined procurement; weakness is that attestations may become perfunctory if not tied to meaningful performance metrics or enforcement.

name: Domestic cryptographic modules and open-source wallet components
consequences: Develop domestic cryptographic primitives and open-source wallet components to reduce reliance on foreign vendors; direct effect is to create local capabilities and regulatory alignment, but it increases initial R&D costs and risk of duplicative efforts.
options: Fund and supervise a government-led development of open-source cryptographic modules and portable identity wallets that implement standard protocols.
Partner with academic labs to accelerate secure, platform-neutral authentication research, open-sourcing results for industry adoption.
Create a staged procurement preference for domestic-source cryptography and compatibility testing to encourage local suppliers.
review: Core tension is balancing domestic capability development with risk of duplication and market fragmentation; weakness is that open-source momentum may not guarantee regulatory acceptance or international interoperability benchmarks.

name: Legal and regulatory resilience pathways for fallback authentication
consequences: Develop regulatory pathways recognizing platform-neutral fallback authentication as a legally valid path for critical public services; this reduces policy risk and aligns with resilience goals. Downstream, it may trigger regulatory friction and requires careful negotiation with EU and Danish authorities to avoid misalignment and delays. It also necessitates harmonization with NSIS and eIDAS2 expectations.
options: Draft legislative amendments recognizing platform-neutral fallbacks as compliant for essential public services and eIDAS2 alignment.
Coordinate EU-aligned policy proposals to embed interoperability mandates in NSIS and eIDAS2 contexts.
Establish a formal contingency framework enabling emergency authorization processes during platform outages.
review: Core tension is aligning national provisions with EU-level interoperability goals; weakness is that regulatory work can become protracted and may stall procurement progress if alignment milestones are not synchronized with phase gates.

name: Copenhagen security-lab for platform-neutral identity testing
consequences: Establishes a secure, Copenhagen-based testing hub to run demonstrators, validate interoperability, and host stakeholder engagements. Direct effect is credibility for policy proposals and technical demonstrations; downstream, it increases ongoing facility costs, security requirements, and scheduling complexity that can slow progression.
options: Establish a secure, Copenhagen-based identity testing lab with controlled access, enabling live demonstrations of fallback authentication workflows.
Host regular multi-stakeholder workshops at the Copenhagen site to align procurement language and policy expectations.
Develop on-site archiving and governance processes to ensure compliance with data protection and security review requirements.
review: Core tension is balancing a centralized demonstration venue with distributed, multi-actor governance needs; weakness is that the lab’s success depends on sustained funding and steady stakeholder engagement to avoid becoming a one-off showcase.

name: Platform-neutral fallback authentication procurement commitments
consequences: Adopting procurement commitments ensures fallback paths are legally required, creating a direct path to platform independence. This creates a clear constraint for vendors and elevates resilience in public services; downstream, vendors will bear additional certification and integration overhead, and procurement cycles may lengthen as interoperability gates are verified.
options: Mandate inclusion of platform-neutral fallback authentication requirements in all AltID-related procurement documents, with explicit evaluation criteria and staged milestones for compatibility.
Create phased procurement lanes that prioritize vendors delivering compliant fallback pathways and standards-based interoperability, with formal gates tied to resilience demonstrations.
Prohibit exclusive distribution arrangements with Apple or Google by inserting vendor-diversity clauses and empowering independent certification bodies to assess trust-anchor independence.
review: Core tension: platform-neutral fallback commitments can slow procurement and invite pushback from incumbents, risking delay in critical capabilities. A missing weakness: the options do not address interim funding for transitional pilots or the governance overhead needed to certify cross-platform trust anchors.

name: Credible non-production demonstrator governance and escalation pathway
consequences: Direct effect: establishes formal governance around non-production demonstrators, confirming security controls, data-handling, and stakeholder escalation. Downstream, this builds credibility with policymakers and partners, but adds oversight burden and potential delays if reviews become bottlenecks.
options: Establish a formal governance charter for non-production demonstrators, including security review, stakeholder sign-off, and escalation channels to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen.
Create staged approval processes with two gates and documented risk acceptance before external demonstrations, ensuring clear accountability.
Publish a transparent demonstration plan that outlines scope, data handling, and non-production environment boundaries to preserve trust and manage expectations.
review: Core tension: non-production demonstrations may overstate resilience benefits without real-world stress testing, and the options miss independent validation steps.

name: Resilience-focused procurement evaluation criteria with supplier diversification clauses
consequences: Procurement scoring emphasizes resilience, fault-tolerance, and incident-response capabilities, plus diversity of supply. Downstream: this may increase bid complexity and cost, and could provoke resistance from incumbents who rely on single-vendor ecosystems.
options: Embed resilience-focused evaluation criteria in procurement scoring, including fallback coverage, incident response alignment, and supplier diversification requirements.
Incorporate supplier diversification clauses that require a mix of domestic and international partners to avoid single-vendor risk concentration.
Adopt a parallel-path evaluation framework that awards non-platform-tethered solutions even if marginally costlier but with higher continuity guarantees.
review: Core tension: diversification can diffuse accountability and complicate integration testing; weakness: the options do not specify post-award monitoring or interoperability verification.

name: EU-level standards advocacy for platform-neutral identity wallets
consequences: Positions Denmark within EU conversations on interoperability and resilience, shaping policy proposals and procurement language. Downstream: commitment to EU standards can slow national decisions if EU consensus lags, and may require compromises on national autonomy.
options: Lead a European standards liaison to advocate platform-neutral identity wallet requirements within the European Digital Identity framework.
Coordinate with NSIS and eIDAS working groups to propose resilience-oriented requirements that reduce platform dependency for member states.
Publish a policy brief and participate in EU fora that articulate fallback authentication pathways as a core interoperability criterion.
review: Core tension: EU-level consensus may overgeneralize requirements, making national procurement language more generic; weakness: the options omit a concrete mapping to Danish procurement templates and oversight.

name: Coalition-based stakeholder pressure and parliamentary engagement framework
consequences: Direct effect: builds political momentum and public legitimacy for platform-neutral fallback work; Downstream: increases political risk exposure if arguments become polarized or if interests mobilize against changes.
options: Formalize a multi-stakeholder coalition with privacy advocates, civil society, and policymakers to draft a joint position paper and timeline for platform-neutral fallback goals.
Schedule regular parliamentary briefings and public hearings to maintain visibility and friction with incumbents while clarifying concrete milestones.
Develop a rapid-response communications protocol that translates technical findings into accessible policy language for Folketinget members.
review: Core tension: political timetables may outpace technical milestones; weakness: the options miss a formal governance mechanism for how coalition recommendations translate into procurement decisions.

name: Fallback authentication governance and certification scheme for critical public services
consequences: Direct effect: creates a formal, auditable certification pathway for fallback authentication, enabling service operators to verify compliance; downstream: requires establishing a new or delegated cert body, budget, and maintenance, potentially delaying rollout.
options: Define an independent fallback-authentication certification framework with clear criteria, test procedures, and audit rights for public service operators.
Commission a neutral testbed to validate platform-neutral fallback pathways against real-world service workflows and user scenarios.
Institute a phased rollout plan for certified fallback access across non-critical services first to verify operations before widespread adoption.
review: Core tension: certification overhead can stall procurement while standards catch up; weakness: the options do not specify funding mechanisms or governance for the cert body.
